title: ついに最高傑作【白子のペペロンチーノ】こんなに美味しいなら毎日食べたいパスタ
channel: ファビオ飯 /イタリア料理人の世界
description:
今が季節の白子の詳しい下処理と魚介系のペペロンチーノの組み合わせについて話しました🍝この白子のマリネをするとスーパーのB級品でも美味しく食べられますよ👨‍🍳

ーーーーーーーーーーーー
◆白子の柚子香るペペロンチーノ◆
食材
リングイーネ70g
白子　40g
黄柚子　適量
ニンニク　ひとかけ　
イタリアンパセリ　4g
カラブリア唐辛子　1本
EXオリーブオイル　大2
鮎の魚醤　10g 麺つゆでも美味です
あさりだし　100cc    ※茹で汁でも可

作り方
①白子の硬い筋をハサミで掃除し真水で洗い、2%の塩水に30分漬け血抜きをする。
②1%の塩水を60°まで上げ白子を2〜3分茹でておく。その後柚子の皮とExオリーブオイルでマリネする。
③1%のパスタ湯でリングイーネを袋表記茹で始める。
④フライパン55Exオイルとニンニクのみじん切りと唐辛子を火にかけじっくりと加熱し火を止め刻んだイタパセと柚子果汁、皮、魚醤、茹で汁を加えておく。
⑤茹で上げたパスタを加え約30秒ソースを吸わせ盛り付ける。
⑤仕上げにマリネした白子と柚子の皮をかけ完成です。

ーーーーーーーーーーーー
【チャプター】
0:00 材料紹介
0:35 白子が見違える下処理
5:24 材料カット
5:58 ソースを作っていく
8:01 麺を合わせていく
9:27 完成・実食
9:51 白子ポン酢が好き

ーーーーーーーーーーーー

#パスタ #ペペロンチーノ #白子のパスタ

ingredients.name: 鮎の魚醤（またはめんつゆ）
あさり出汁（または茹で汁）

steps.text: 白子の硬い筋をハサミで取り除き真水で洗い、2%の塩水に30分漬けて血抜きをする。60度のお湯で2〜3分茹でて、柚子の皮とオリーブオイルでマリネする。
steps.point: 白子の血抜きは臭み取りに必須の工程。2%塩水は白子に対して浸透圧が働き、血液を引き出す。60度の低温茹ではプリプリの食感を保ちながらちょうど良い火入れになる。柚子でマリネすることで磯の香りが和らぎ柑橘の爽やかさが加わる。

steps.text: フライパンにオリーブオイル・ニンニクのみじん切り・唐辛子を弱火でじっくり加熱し、火を止めてパセリ・柚子果汁・柚子の皮・魚醤を加える。
steps.point: ペペロンチーノベースに柚子果汁と魚醤を加えることで和洋折衷のソースが完成する。魚醤がうま味の底上げをしてあさり出汁との相乗効果を生む。火を止めた後に加えることで繊細な柚子の香りが飛ばない。

steps.text: あさり出汁を加えてソースを整え、茹で上がったパスタを加えて約30秒ソースを吸わせる。
steps.point: あさり出汁が全体の旨味を一段上げる。パスタをソースに入れて30秒程度吸わせることでパスタの芯までソースの旨味が浸透する。リングイーネの幅広の形状がソースをよく受け止める。

steps.text: 盛り付けてマリネした白子と柚子の皮を飾って完成。
steps.point: 白子はトッピングとして最後に乗せることで滑らかなクリーミーなテクスチャーが活きる。加熱しないため白子本来のとろけるような食感が楽しめる。柚子の皮の黄色が美しい仕上がりに。

subtitles.en:
Two people can fit inside. Wow, it looks
delicious.
In winter, I
want to eat it almost every day. Hi, hello, this is
Chef Famile. Today we're going to
make cod milt peperoncino. Okay, so let's
introduce the ingredients. Rings are good. Cod milt,
garlic, chili peppers,
Italian parsley, cucumber, and fish sauce.
Okay, so as part of our peperoncino
series, and because it's the season for it, we're going to
make peperoncino using cod milt. This is so incredibly
delicious, so let's start
by preparing the cod milt. Let's get started
cleaning the cod milt. This is
typical cod milt. This is also
something I bought at the supermarket. When you
buy it at the supermarket, first fill this bowl with
water, preferably with ice, and
add about 1% salt, then soak the cod milt in it. Cod
milt has a unique smell, so soaking it for about
an hour removes that smell.
This removes a certain amount of the smell, so
changing the water as you go is the best way to do it. When you hold the
cod milt like this, it forms one lump.  It's
roughly done, and
what we need to remove from this mass is
this surface, and if you turn it over, there are lots of
hard tendons here, you can
see this right away, and if you grab the tendons it looks like this, and we'll
remove only these hard tendons. You can remove them with a knife,
but the method I use and
recommend the most is scissors. Turn it over with scissors and
grab the tendons, and once you've grabbed it, the
milt will go down, so just use the
scissors to cut the hard tendons on top, snip, snip. It's not
that difficult, so you just
need to remove the hard, small pieces, and some
people who are a little meticulous tend to do this quite often,
but if you do that, the milt will fall apart too much,
so as long as you remove the hard tendons, there's no problem,
just use scissors to cut the parts that are sticking out a little, like this. Once the
hard tendons are removed, we
want to cut it into bite-sized pieces, so
To avoid breaking the milt, there are these
tiny fibers, so cut along them. That's how you do it. Use your skill to cut through them. This will make it form
one solid mass, so leave it like this. Now we're going to parboil the milt.
Add about 2% salt to the water in the pot, and just like with pasta, you season it while you
boil it. The
reason for parboiling the milt is that raw milt usually
contains Anisakis parasites, so parboiling
kills them. Also, parboiling makes it firmer.
Boil it at a good temperature. The ideal
temperature is around 70 to
80 degrees Celsius, with 60 degrees being ideal for
killing the Anisakis and making it nice and firm.
To further remove any fishy smell from the milt, you can add a little
white wine.
Today I'm adding sake, but if you're
drinking white wine, you can
add something like that like this. Today I'm using
sake.  The
water in the pot is now around 70-75 degrees Celsius, so we're going to
put the cod milt in. We'll put it in for about
23 minutes. It's more like letting the
cod milt swim around rather than boiling it. We're
not turning on the heat right now, so we're just turning it around a bit while we boil the
cod milt. This way, we can also
remove the slime and dirt from the cod milt. You
could do it quickly in boiling water,
but I
think the best state for cod milt is when it's plump, so we're going to let it
swim around at 70-80 degrees. The
cod milt has been boiled for 23 minutes, so look at
this
plumpness. This would be
great with cod milt ponzu sauce. Now we'll
wipe off the moisture thoroughly with a paper towel. Now we've wiped off the moisture from the
cod milt and put it in.
Now it's winter so we're using
Q's cheese with a leathery aroma, and a
mild-smelling
olive oil. By
marinating the cod milt with this, it will be delicious.  We've got some amazing
shirako (cod milt) with absolutely no fishy smell and a wonderful aroma, so we're going to put it on
top of the pasta. We'll
put it on top of the pasta as a finishing touch, and it's
delicious with soy sauce or ponzu sauce, so I'm using a little extra today. Now we'll
make the pasta sauce. Remove the core from the garlic and mince it. Chop the
parsley to your liking. Today's is a
violin base, so if you don't have
parsley, honey would also be delicious. We'll also
use a little yuzu juice, so cut it up.Okay, we've
bought the ingredients, so now we'll make the
sauce. Just add the garlic and
chili peppers to
make the base. Then we'll
heat it up. Now the
garlic smells great and it's simmering, so we'll cook the noodles. Today we're using shirako, which is seafood, and
linguine goes well with seafood.  The
rings look good, let's boil them.
Okay, the garlic has started to
brown slightly, so now we'll add the clam broth.
If you don't have clam broth, you can
use the boiling water, but
if you use the boiling water, it might
lack some umami, so in that case, try
adding a little mentsuyu or fish sauce to
adjust it. The reason we're
adding clam broth today is a
basic
technique used in restaurants. Today we have a seafood
pasta with cod roe, so for
example, when making pasta with other fish fillets or anything else, when you use a
base,
adding a broth with similar characteristics really
improves the compatibility, so today we're
adding clam broth. It's
absolutely delicious when you use clams, but it's also
delicious if you use the boiling water. Once it
boils,
taste the base once. It's a
very delicate broth, so add a little
fish sauce for umami. We
also want the aroma of yuzu,
so add a little yuzu as well.
Squeeze out the excess water and
wait a bit for the noodles to finish
cooking. They'll be ready soon, so turn the
heat back on and add the toppings.
Today, we're using clam broth, and I
want the noodles to absorb the delicious flavor of the broth, so I've
cooked them a little al dente, about 30 seconds before the end. The sauce is also quite runny right now,
so add the noodles to it. And
cook it for another minute or so and it will be just right, so
cook it from here.
Then add the Italian parsley. Wow, that looks
delicious! I'd
want to eat this almost every day in the winter. Okay, so it's been
cooked for a minute now, and the
surface moisture is almost gone. Turn off the
heat.
Turn it off and mix it at the end to thicken it, so once it's done, we'll
plate it. The
cod roe peperoncino is complete! Let's eat! Wow,
this is amazing! The creaminess
of the cod roe combined with the aioli is
just the best! It's so
good!
Shirako is one of my personal favorite ingredients, so
every year when winter comes around, I
make a lot of dishes with it. My
favorite is
shirako with ponzu sauce, but I
think there are many other ways to prepare it, like frying, boiling, or grilling, so please
tell me your favorite way to cook shirako. Yes, thank you for the meal.
This shirako pasta is the
best in my peperoncino series,
so please try making it if you like. Yes,
thank you for your comments as always. I always
enjoy reading them. Yes,
see you in the next video.
Bye.
